challenges.title: Fix the Broken Authentication
challenges.description: Users are reporting they cannot log in. The authentication middleware is failing silently — valid tokens are being rejected and the server crashes on missing tokens. Identify every bug and fix them. Consider error handling, token format, and security best practices.

hidden_tests.description: Returns 200 and user data when token is valid
Does not crash on missing Authorization header

bugs_to_find: No try/catch around jwt.verify — crashes on invalid tokens
No check for missing authorization header

challenges.title: Two Sum
challenges.description: Given an array of integers and a target sum, return the indices of the two numbers that add up to the target. Each input has exactly one solution. You may not use the same element twice. Expected time complexity: O(n).

challenges.title: Valid Parentheses
challenges.description: Given a string containing only '(', ')', '{', '}', '[', ']', determine if the input string is valid. Brackets must close in the correct order.

challenges.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
challenges.description: Given a string, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters. This is a classic sliding window problem asked at Google, Amazon, and Meta.

challenges.title: Maximum Subarray (Kadane's Algorithm)
challenges.description: Given an integer array, find the contiguous subarray with the largest sum and return its sum. This is one of the most frequently asked Amazon interview questions.

challenges.title: Binary Search Tree — Validate
challenges.description: Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree. A valid BST requires all left subtree values to be less than the node, and all right subtree values to be greater.

challenges.title: Number of Islands
challenges.description: Given a 2D grid of '1's (land) and '0's (water), count the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically. Classic Google/Amazon BFS/DFS question.

challenges.title: Merge K Sorted Lists
challenges.description: You are given an array of k linked lists, each sorted in ascending order. Merge all the linked lists into one sorted linked list. This is a classic hard-level question asked at Google, Meta, and Microsoft.

challenges.title: Word Break II
challenges.description: Given a string s and a dictionary of strings wordDict, add spaces in s to construct a sentence where each word is a valid dictionary word. Return all such possible sentences. Asked at Google and Amazon for senior roles.

challenges.title: Trapping Rain Water
challenges.description: Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining. One of the most iconic hard problems asked at every top MNC.

challenges.title: Race Condition in Order Processing
challenges.description: An e-commerce platform is occasionally processing the same order twice, charging customers double. The bug is a race condition in the order processing service. Identify and fix it using proper locking or idempotency patterns.

hidden_tests.description: Concurrent calls with same orderId only charge once

bugs_to_find: Race condition between has() check and add()
Solution: optimistic lock or DB-level unique constraint or mutex

challenges.title: SQL Injection Vulnerability
challenges.description: A user search endpoint is vulnerable to SQL injection. An attacker can manipulate the query to dump the entire users table or bypass authentication. Identify the vulnerability and fix it using parameterized queries.

hidden_tests.description: Injection payload returns no extra rows

expected_outcomes.fix: Use parameterized queries: db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = $1', [username])

challenges.title: Broken Access Control — IDOR
challenges.description: Users can access other users' private data by changing the ID in the URL. This is an Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) vulnerability — one of the OWASP Top 10. Fix the endpoint to enforce ownership checks.

hidden_tests.description: User cannot access another user's document
User can access their own document

expected_outcomes.fix: Add WHERE user_id = req.user.id to the query

challenges.title: N+1 Query Problem
challenges.description: An API endpoint that lists blog posts with their authors is making N+1 database queries — one for the posts and one per post for the author. This causes severe performance issues at scale. Fix it using a JOIN or eager loading.

hidden_tests.description: Returns posts with authors in a single query
All posts have author data populated

expected_outcomes.fix: Use JOIN: SELECT posts.*, users.name as author_name FROM posts JOIN users ON posts.user_id = users.id

challenges.title: Optimize Slow Search Endpoint
challenges.description: A product search endpoint is timing out under load. It fetches all products from the database, filters in JavaScript, then sorts in memory. With 500K products this is catastrophically slow. Optimize it.

expected_outcomes.fix: Push filtering and sorting into SQL with WHERE, ILIKE, and ORDER BY. Add indexes on category and price.

challenges.title: Untangle the God Function
challenges.description: A 120-line function handles user registration: validates input, hashes password, creates user, sends welcome email, logs the event, and returns a response — all in one place. Refactor it into clean, single-responsibility modules without changing behaviour.

hidden_tests.description: Registration still works end to end

expected_outcomes.approach: Extract validateInput, hashPassword, createUser, sendWelcomeEmail into separate modules

challenges.title: Design a Rate Limiter
challenges.description: Implement a rate limiting middleware that allows a maximum of 100 requests per IP per minute. Requests exceeding the limit should return HTTP 429. This is a classic system design implementation question asked at Uber, Stripe, and Cloudflare.

expected_outcomes.approach: Sliding window counter or token bucket with in-memory Map or Redis

challenges.title: Implement a Job Queue
challenges.description: Build a simple in-memory job queue system that supports: enqueue(job), a worker that processes jobs one at a time, retry logic (max 3 attempts on failure), and a dead letter queue for permanently failed jobs. Asked at Amazon and Microsoft for backend roles.

hidden_tests.description: Failing job is retried 3 times then dead-lettered
Dead letter queue contains failed job

expected_outcomes.approach: Queue array + retry counter per job + dead letter array

challenges.title: Coin Change (Dynamic Programming)
challenges.description: Given an array of coin denominations and a target amount, return the minimum number of coins needed to make up that amount. If it cannot be made, return -1. Classic DP question asked at Google, Amazon, and Microsoft.

expected_outcomes.approach: Bottom-up DP with dp array of size amount+1

challenges.title: Longest Common Subsequence
challenges.description: Given two strings, return the length of their longest common subsequence. A subsequence is a sequence that appears in the same relative order but not necessarily contiguous. Core DP problem asked at Google and Meta.

challenges.title: Course Schedule (Cycle Detection)
challenges.description: There are numCourses courses labeled 0 to n-1. Given prerequisites pairs [a,b] meaning you must take b before a, determine if it is possible to finish all courses. This is cycle detection in a directed graph — asked at Google and Facebook.

expected_outcomes.approach: Topological sort or DFS cycle detection

challenges.title: Word Ladder
challenges.description: Given two words and a word list, find the length of the shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord, where each step changes exactly one letter and each intermediate word must exist in the word list. BFS hard question — asked at Amazon and Google.

challenges.title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
challenges.description: A sorted array has been rotated at an unknown pivot. Given a target value, return its index or -1 if not found. Must run in O(log n). Asked at almost every MNC — Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Adobe.

challenges.title: Find Median from Data Stream
challenges.description: Design a data structure that supports adding numbers from a data stream and finding the median at any point. This is a classic two-heap problem asked at Google and Apple for senior engineering roles.

expected_outcomes.approach: Max-heap + min-heap, rebalance on each insert

challenges.title: Design LRU Cache
challenges.description: Design a data structure that follows the constraints of a Least Recently Used (LRU) cache. Implement LRUCache class with get(key) and put(key, value) in O(1) time complexity.

conversation.content: Hi, let's fix the bugs in middleware/auth.js. Please inspect it and propose a fix.

content.reasoning: The current implementation of the authenticate middleware in middleware/auth.js does not handle cases where the 'authorization' header is missing or where the token is invalid. This could lead to runtime errors when trying to verify the token or access the 'user' property of the request object. We need to add error handling to ensure the middleware behaves correctly in these scenarios.

proposed_changes.replacement: const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, (err, decoded) => { if (err) { return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid token' }); } return decoded; });
proposed_changes.explanation: However, jwt.verify is a synchronous function and does not take a callback. Instead, we should use a try-catch block to catch any errors that occur during verification.

proposed_changes.replacement: const token = req.headers['authorization']; if (!token) { return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Token is missing' }); }
proposed_changes.explanation: We should check if the token exists before trying to verify it to prevent runtime errors.

proposed_changes.replacement: try { const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET); req.user = decoded; next(); } catch (error) { return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid token' }); }
proposed_changes.explanation: Using a try-catch block to catch any errors that occur during token verification and returning a 401 response if an error occurs.

content.follow_up: Test the updated authenticate middleware to ensure it correctly handles missing or invalid tokens and that it successfully verifies valid tokens.

conversation.content: Hi, let's fix the bugs in middleware/auth.js. Please inspect it and propose a fix.

content.reasoning: The authenticate function in middleware/auth.js does not handle cases where the 'authorization' header is missing or the token is invalid. This could lead to runtime errors. We need to add error handling for jwt.verify and check if the token exists before trying to verify it.

proposed_changes.replacement: const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, (err, decoded) => { if (err) { return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Invalid token' }); } req.user = decoded; next(); });
proposed_changes.explanation: However, jwt.verify is a synchronous function and does not support callbacks. We should use try-catch block instead to handle the error.
Add a null check for the token to prevent runtime errors when the 'authorization' header is missing.

proposed_changes.replacement: try { const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET); req.user = decoded; next(); } catch (err) { return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Invalid token' }); }
proposed_changes.explanation: Use a try-catch block to catch any errors that occur during token verification and return a 401 status code with an error message.

content.follow_up: Test the updated authenticate function with different scenarios, such as a valid token, an invalid token, and a missing 'authorization' header, to ensure it behaves as expected.

content.reasoning: The provided code snippet is for authentication middleware using JSON Web Tokens (JWT). It extracts the token from the 'Authorization' header, verifies it using a secret key stored in the environment variable 'JWT_SECRET', and then assigns the decoded token to the 'user' property of the request object. However, this code does not handle potential errors that might occur during the verification process, such as an invalid or missing token.

proposed_changes.replacement: const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, (err, decoded) => { if (err) { return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Invalid token' }); } req.user = decoded; next(); });
proposed_changes.explanation: This change adds error handling for the JWT verification process. If an error occurs (e.g., the token is invalid or missing), it returns a 401 status with an error message. Otherwise, it proceeds as before, assigning the decoded token to the request object and calling the next middleware function.

content.follow_up: The candidate should consider implementing additional error handling and logging mechanisms to improve the robustness and security of the authentication middleware. They should also ensure that the 'JWT_SECRET' environment variable is properly set and secured in their application.

content.reasoning: The current implementation of the authenticate middleware does not handle cases where the 'authorization' header is missing or the token is invalid. This could lead to runtime errors. We need to add error handling to ensure the middleware behaves correctly in these scenarios.

proposed_changes.replacement: jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, (err, decoded) => { if (err) { return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Invalid token' }); } req.user = decoded; next(); });
proposed_changes.explanation: Using the callback version of jwt.verify allows us to catch and handle any errors that occur during token verification, such as an invalid token or a missing 'authorization' header.
Adding a fallback to check for 'Authorization' (with a capital 'A') ensures the middleware works with both lowercase and uppercase header names.

content.follow_up: Test the updated authenticate middleware with different scenarios, including valid and invalid tokens, and missing 'authorization' headers, to ensure it behaves as expected.

conversation.content: The current code has duplicate next() calls and doesn't handle the token correctly. Please write a completely clean middleware/auth.js from scratch. Use a synchronous try-catch block around jwt.verify, check for a missing authorization header, handle the 'Bearer ' prefix, and return a 401 response if verification fails."

content.reasoning: The current implementation of the authenticate middleware has several issues, including duplicate next() calls and incorrect token handling. A clean implementation should use a synchronous try-catch block around jwt.verify, check for a missing 'authorization' header, handle the 'Bearer ' prefix, and return a 401 response if verification fails.

proposed_changes.explanation: This implementation first checks for the presence and correctness of the 'authorization' header, then extracts the token and verifies it using jwt.verify. If verification fails, it returns a 401 response. If successful, it sets the req.user property and calls next().

content.follow_up: Test the updated authenticate middleware with different scenarios, including valid and invalid tokens, and missing or malformed 'authorization' headers, to ensure it behaves as expected.

content.reasoning: The twoSum function is not implemented. It should iterate over the array of numbers and find two numbers that add up to the target. We can use a hash map to store the numbers we have seen so far and their indices.

proposed_changes.original: function twoSum(nums, target) { // Your solution here }
proposed_changes.explanation: This change implements the twoSum function using a hash map to store the numbers we have seen so far and their indices. It iterates over the array of numbers and for each number, it checks if its complement (target - number) is in the hash map. If it is, it returns the indices of the complement and the current number. If not, it adds the current number and its index to the hash map.

content.follow_up: Test the twoSum function with different inputs to ensure it is working correctly. For example, twoSum([2, 7, 11, 15], 9) should return [0, 1].

conversation.content: Please implement the optimal O(n) solution for Two Sum in solution.js. Use a Map to keep track of seen numbers and their indices, find the complement of each number relative to the target, and return the correct pair of indices. Replace the "// Your solution here" comment inside the function.

content.reasoning: The optimal solution for the Two Sum problem involves using a Map to store the numbers we have seen so far and their indices. We then iterate over the array, and for each number, we calculate its complement relative to the target. If the complement is already in the Map, we return the current index and the index of the complement. If not, we add the current number and its index to the Map. This approach allows us to find the solution in O(n) time complexity.

proposed_changes.explanation: This replacement implements the optimal O(n) solution for the Two Sum problem using a Map to keep track of seen numbers and their indices.

content.follow_up: Test the function with example inputs, such as twoSum([2, 7, 11, 15], 9), to verify that it returns the correct pair of indices, which is [0, 1].
